next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

The lodge room was above a tire shop and beside the bus stand, so the whole building kept shuddering under other people's departures. Each time a coach backed out below, the window glass rattled in its frame and the iron cots gave a small answering tremor, as if the room itself wanted to climb down and leave. The plaster had once been painted a clean blue. Sweat, handprints, and years of damp had turned it the color of old dishwater. A fan with one bent blade churned the hot air without improving it.

Halima set the rolled bedding on the nearer cot and stood a moment with both hands on the bundle, letting her back unhook one knot at a time. The day had been all ferries, office benches, one clerk's face after another, Aminul's careful voice, Nura's silence stretched so tight it had begun to feel like a second strap cutting across the room. She had thought the waiting itself would save her. People on the mainland were always making them wait. A waiting day ate questions. A missed launch, a missing signature, a file sent upstairs and not returned, all of that could carry a person until evening without the person having to say the thing she meant not to say.

Now there was nowhere for the day to go. The office had shut. The last boat back had gone. Aminul had found this room because the cheaper ones downstairs rented by the hour and he would not leave two women in those. That had been plain in the way he did not look at the desk clerk while he counted the notes out. He had only said, with that same office politeness, that he had taken the room across the corridor...
open_hooks_to_carry: Travel off the char changes scale and shows the institutional contempt surrounding displacement.
The records office demonstrates that bureaucratic violence can be cold rather than loud.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d the day's heat and gave it back slowly. From far off, toward the ghat, a ferry engine coughed and caught. Someone in the lane began reciting prayer in a hoarse voice, not from piety exactly but because routine felt like protection after a night when walls and weather had both gone loose. Halima slid the oilcloth packet behind the rice jar again and sat with the exercise pages in her lap a little longer, reading what she and Nura had salvaged from memory.

The missing leaves no longer felt like the blind aftermath of a storm. They felt pointed. Selected. A hand had entered her house through rumor and male license, then left carrying only the pieces that could fatten a claim on dry ground. That changed the injury. It also changed the work. Rage would only make noise. Proof would need patience, comparison, and a man greedy enough to show the shape of what he had stolen.

She folded the exercise pages small and tucked them under the lamp base where even floodwater would have to rise high to reach. Then she called to Nura to start letting people collect what had survived. There would be questions. There would be faces measuring her while they took back their damp lives. She was not ready for accusation and not interested in defense. Not yet.

When the first knock came on the post, Halima put her palm flat on the charpoy rope and felt how much water still lived inside the house. Then she stood, wiped her hand on her sari, and went to answer it thinking not of outrage now, but of east-side names, of copied leaves, of the order in which a greedy man might try to make them useful.
state_deltas_required: Find an administrative route that can protect households whose blood records will never reconcile cleanly.
Halima learns that a household-occupancy affidavit route exists, but it will require public testimony that exposes private histories.
